examples.title: LCP Configure-Request Processing
examples.description: Algorithm showing how LCP handles configuration requests in PPP
examples.code:
PROCEDURE LCP_Handle_Configure_Request(packet)
BEGIN
 IF packet.options IS acceptable THEN
 SEND Configure-Ack(packet.options)
 state ← Opened
 ELSE IF packet.options CAN be negotiated THEN
 SEND Configure-Nak(packet.acceptable_options)
 ELSE
 SEND Configure-Reject(packet.unacceptable_options)
 ENDIF
END
examples.explanation: When PPP receives a configure-request from peer, it either acknowledges acceptable options, suggests alternatives via Nak, or rejects unsupported options. This is the core of link negotiation.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of options

examples.title: CHAP Authentication Algorithm
examples.description: Three-way handshake authentication process in CHAP
examples.code:
// SERVER SIDE
PROCEDURE CHAP_Challenge()
BEGIN
 challenge_id ← generate_random()
 challenge_value ← generate_random_bytes(16)
 SEND Challenge(challenge_id, challenge_value)
 STORE challenge_id, challenge_value, peer_name
END

// CLIENT SIDE
PROCEDURE CHAP_Response(challenge_id, challenge_value)
BEGIN
 hash ← MD5(challenge_value + password)
 SEND Response(challenge_id, hash, client_name)
END

// SERVER VERIFICATION
PROCEDURE CHAP_Verify(response)
BEGIN
 expected_hash ← MD5(stored_challenge + stored_password)
 IF response.hash == expected_hash THEN
 SEND Success(response.id)
 ELSE
 SEND Failure(response.id)
 ENDIF
END
examples.explanation: CHAP uses a three-way handshake: Server sends random challenge, Client responds with MD5 hash of challenge+password, Server verifies and responds. The password is never sent over the link.

examples.title: PPP Frame Flag Detection and Escaping
examples.description: Algorithm for detecting and escaping special characters in PPP
examples.code:
PROCEDURE PPP_Transmit_Byte(byte)
BEGIN
 IF byte == FLAG (0x7E) THEN
 OUTPUT ESCAPE (0x7D)
 OUTPUT byte XOR 0x20 // becomes 0x5E
 ELSE IF byte == ESCAPE (0x7D) THEN
 OUTPUT ESCAPE (0x7D)
 OUTPUT byte XOR 0x20 // becomes 0x5D
 ELSE IF byte < 0x20 THEN
 OUTPUT ESCAPE (0x7D)
 OUTPUT byte XOR 0x20
 ELSE
 OUTPUT byte
 ENDIF
END

PROCEDURE PPP_Receive_Byte(byte)
BEGIN
 IF byte == ESCAPE (0x7D) THEN
 next_byte ← READ_NEXT()
 restored_byte ← next_byte XOR 0x20
 OUTPUT restored_byte
 ELSE
 OUTPUT byte
 ENDIF
END
examples.explanation: PPP uses character escaping for async links to ensure the flag sequence (0x7E) never appears inside the frame data. All special characters are escaped with 0x7D and XORed with 0x20.
